paper.title: LIMO: Low-Power In-Memory-Annealer and Matrix-Multiplication Primitive for Edge Computing
paper.abstract: Combinatorial optimization (CO) underpins applications in science and engineering, ranging from logistics to electronic design automation. A classic example is the NP-complete Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP). Finding exact solutions for large-scale TSP instances remains computationally intractable; on von Neumann architectures, such solvers are constrained by the memory wall, incurring compute-memory traffic that grows with instance size. Metaheuristics, such as simulated annealing implemented

memory_insight.before_after_comparisons: 85,900 cities instance solved with superior solution quality and faster time-to-solution

analysis.engineering_takeaway: Using in-memory annealing with stochastic switching of spin-transfer-torque magnetic-tunnel-junctions (STT-MTJs) can improve solution quality for large-scale combinatorial optimization problems
analysis.deployment_feasibility: Feasible on laptop and possibly mobile with sufficient RAM, but actual deployment may be limited by hardware acceleration requirements and power consumption

review_questions: Is this genuinely a breakthrough? Or incremental improvement?
